### Post by DougieFresh4U on 2010-09-28
I know that this [thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1583508") was here already today but I am still having a bit of a problem.
When I run **sudo grub-mkconfig** I get:

```
dougie@Alpha64Bit:~$ sudo grub-mkconfig
Generating grub.cfg ...
#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  set have_grubenv=true
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ "${prev_saved_entry}" ]; then
  set saved_entry="${prev_saved_entry}"
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then
    saved_entry="${chosen}"
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n "${have_grubenv}" ]; then if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}

function load_video {
  insmod vbe
  insmod vga
  insmod video_bochs
  insmod video_cirrus
}

insmod part_msdos
/usr/sbin/grub-probe: error: not a directory.
dougie@Alpha64Bit:~$ 
```
This is a 'triple' boot machine with **WinXP - Lucid - Maverick**and it looks to me as no installs are being seen.
There is definitely a problem here.
It is an issue with the grub-pc update mentioned in the link posted

### Post by drs305 on 2010-09-28
A straight reinstall doesn't always repair things, such as restoring files that the user may have deleted.

I'd recommend purging and reinstalling Grub2. It only takes a couple of minutes and as long as you have a steady internet connection and power supply it's a reliable method of restoring Grub.

I wrote a guide on purging/reinstalling G2 using chroot. If you have to boot from the LiveCD, use the entire procedure. If you can boot into your normal Ubuntu installation, just complete Steps 2 through 5 and use "sudo" with all the commands.

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1581099]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1581099")

---

### Post by ranch hand on 2010-09-28
I would do this.  I am assuming that Lucid will boot itself and Win JerryLewis Pro.

Use these directions and reinstall Lucids grub on the MBR;

[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Grub2#Recover%20Grub%202%20via%20LiveCD](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Grub2#Recover%20Grub%202%20via%20LiveCD)

Ignore any file editing.  Do run update-grub and grub-install.  This should give you a usable menu.

It is much easier to use apt and purge grub-pc and grub-common, delete /boot/grub and reinstall grub-pc aand grub-common from in the OS than out.

---

### Post by drs305 on 2010-09-28
Continued:
Did the purge command appear to work correctly? 

We'll back up a step and consider that there may still be remnants of grub-pc files still on the system, since APT doesn't want to play nice with grub-pc. Since it previously had a problem with the post-installation script, you can attempt a 'dirty' fix by removing APT's access to that script. If it doesn't work, you can give APT access again. If the file was successfully purged, you'll get an error message.

```

sudo mv /var/lib/dpkg/info/grub-pc.postinst /var/lib/dpkg/info/grub-pc.postinst.bad
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade

```

If that clears things up, I would still accomplish the purge/reinstall.

If it doesn't fix it, change the name back.
```

sudo mv /var/lib/dpkg/info/grub-pc.postinst.bad /var/lib/dpkg/info/grub-pc.postinst

```

You also might find out what APT thinks the status of grub-pc is with this command:
```

grep -A1 "Package: grub-pc" /var/lib/dpkg/status

```

### Post by drs305 on 2010-09-28
Since grub-pc is broken, let's just remove it from APT's memory.

Remove all grub-pc files from /var/lib/dpkg/info. Open your browser as root and navigate to /var/lib/dpkg/info.  Move all the "grub-pc" files (about 10) to the root Desktop (you could delete them but I prefer moving them initially).

Next, remove the reference from the *status* file:
```

sudo cp /var/lib/dpkg/status /var/lib/dpkg/status.my.old
gksu gedit /var/lib/dpkg/status
```

Find "Package: grub-pc" and remove the entire section - from "Package: grub-pc" to the blank line before the next "Package: ..." entry. Save the file.

If you have an Internet connection, you may want to empty your package cache in case a downloaded corrupt grub-pc package is lurking in your archive (sudo apt-get clean).  Run "sudo apt-get update" and then try the grub-pc install again.

### Post by VMC on 2010-09-29
Run Boot_Info_Script(see my blue link below for download location), so we can get a more complete picture of your partitions, grub, mbr, etc.

The results of running that script is found in a file named RESULTS.txt

### Post by dino99 on 2010-09-29
the rough way:

open nautilus and search for "grub" into the system folders, this will return all the related files about grub: delete them all

then check your sources.list (synaptic repo tab): be sure you only use genuine repo for the same release (maverick), maybe some lucid too like medibuntu. Deactivate all the exotic ppa if any.

Update then install grub-pc

this should work now

---

```
#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by /usr/sbin/grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ ${prev_saved_entry} ]; then
  set saved_entry=${prev_saved_entry}
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=

    save_env saved_entry

  set recordfail=1

}
insmod ext2
set root='(hd0,7)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 22ef8303-6e22-435b-9357-0a725d14cff8
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=640x480
  insmod gfxterm
  insmod vbe
  if terminal_output gfxterm ; then true ; else
    # For backward compatibility with versions of terminal.mod that don't
    # understand terminal_output
    terminal gfxterm
  fi
fi
insmod ext2
set root='(hd0,7)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 22ef8303-6e22-435b-9357-0a725d14cff8
set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
set lang=en
insmod gettext
if [ ${recordfail} = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.32-25-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {

#
# Use 'blkid -o value -s UUID' to print the universally unique identifier
# for a device; this may be used with UUID= as a more robust way to name
# devices that works even if disks are added and removed. See fstab(5).
#
```

### Post by sdowney717 on 2010-09-29
here is mine for comparison
I noticed the first bit of info on yours seems to point to an error

>  => Grub 2 is installed in the MBR of /dev/sda and looks at sector 523349784 
    of the same hard drive for core.img, but core.img can not be found at this 
    location.



```

=========================== sda5/boot/grub/menu.lst: ===========================

# menu.lst - See: grub(8), info grub, update-grub(8)
#            grub-install(8), grub-floppy(8),
#            grub-md5-crypt, /usr/share/doc/grub
#            and /usr/share/doc/grub-doc/.

## default num
# Set the default entry to the entry number NUM. Numbering starts from 0, and
# the entry number 0 is the default if the command is not used.
#
# You can specify 'saved' instead of a number. In this case, the default entry
# is the entry saved with the command 'savedefault'.
# WARNING: If you are using dmraid do not use 'savedefault' or your
# array will desync and will not let you boot your system.
default        0

## timeout sec
# Set a timeout, in SEC seconds, before automatically booting the default entry
# (normally the first entry defined).
timeout        10

## hiddenmenu
# Hides the menu by default (press ESC to see the menu)
#hiddenmenu

# Pretty colours
#color cyan/blue white/blue

## password ['--md5'] passwd
# If used in the first section of a menu file, disable all interactive editing
# control (menu entry editor and command-line)  and entries protected by the
# command 'lock'
# e.g. password topsecret
#      password --md5 $1$gLhU0/$aW78kHK1QfV3P2b2znUoe/
# password topsecret

#
# examples
#
# title        Windows 95/98/NT/2000
# root        (hd0,0)
# makeactive
# chainloader    +1
#
# title        Linux
# root        (hd0,1)
# kernel    /vmlinuz root=/dev/hda2 ro
#

#
# Put static boot stanzas before and/or after AUTOMAGIC KERNEL LIST

### BEGIN AUTOMAGIC KERNELS LIST
## lines between the AUTOMAGIC KERNELS LIST markers will be modified
## by the debian update-grub script except for the default options below

## DO NOT UNCOMMENT THEM, Just edit them to your needs

## ## Start Default Options ##
## default kernel options
## default kernel options for automagic boot options
## If you want special options for specific kernels use kopt_x_y_z
## where x.y.z is kernel version. Minor versions can be omitted.
## e.g. kopt=root=/dev/hda1 ro
##      kopt_2_6_8=root=/dev/hdc1 ro
##      kopt_2_6_8_2_686=root=/dev/hdc2 ro
# kopt=root=UUID=4923760a-6e34-4678-b0ab-d06b151a2998 ro

## default grub root device
## e.g. groot=(hd0,0)
# groot=4923760a-6e34-4678-b0ab-d06b151a2998

## should update-grub create alternative automagic boot options
## e.g. alternative=true
##      alternative=false
# alternative=true

## should update-grub lock alternative automagic boot options
## e.g. lockalternative=true
##      lockalternative=false
# lockalternative=false

## additional options to use with the default boot option, but not with the
## alternatives
## e.g. defoptions=vga=791 resume=/dev/hda5
# defoptions=quiet splash

## should update-grub lock old automagic boot options
## e.g. lockold=false
##      lockold=true
# lockold=false

## Xen hypervisor options to use with the default Xen boot option
# xenhopt=

## Xen Linux kernel options to use with the default Xen boot option
# xenkopt=console=tty0

## altoption boot targets option
## multiple altoptions lines are allowed
## e.g. altoptions=(extra menu suffix) extra boot options
##      altoptions=(recovery) single
# altoptions=(recovery mode) single

## controls how many kernels should be put into the menu.lst
## only counts the first occurence of a kernel, not the
## alternative kernel options
## e.g. howmany=all
##      howmany=7
# howmany=all

## specify if running in Xen domU or have grub detect automatically
## update-grub will ignore non-xen kernels when running in domU and vice versa

##      indomU=false

# memtest86=true

## should update-grub adjust the value of the default booted system

# savedefault=false

=========================== sda5/boot/grub/grub.cfg: ===========================

#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  set have_grubenv=true
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ "${prev_saved_entry}" ]; then
  set saved_entry="${prev_saved_entry}"
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then
    saved_entry="${chosen}"
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n "${have_grubenv}" ]; then if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}

function load_video {
  insmod vbe
  insmod vga
  insmod video_bochs
  insmod video_cirrus
}

insmod part_msdos
insmod ext2
set root='(hd0,msdos5)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 4923760a-6e34-4678-b0ab-d06b151a2998
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=640x480
  load_video
  insmod gfxterm
fi
terminal_output gfxterm
insmod part_msdos
insmod ext2
set root='(hd0,msdos5)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 4923760a-6e34-4678-b0ab-d06b151a2998
set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
set lang=en
insmod gettext
if [ "${recordfail}" = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.35-22-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    recordfail
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd0,msdos5)'

    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-17-generic
}

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd0,msdos5)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 4923760a-6e34-4678-b0ab-d06b151a2998
    linux16    /boot/memtest86+.bin
}
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+, serial console 115200)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd0,msdos5)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 4923760a-6e34-4678-b0ab-d06b151a2998
    linux16    /boot/memtest86+.bin console=ttyS0,115200n8
}
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Windows Vista (loader) (on /dev/sda1)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 26e82022e81feeb5
    chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
### END /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###
if [ -f  $prefix/custom.cfg ]; then
  source $prefix/custom.cfg;
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###

=============================== sda5/etc/fstab: ===============================

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
# Use 'blkid -o value -s UUID' to print the universally unique identifier
# for a device; this may be used with UUID= as a more robust way to name
# devices that works even if disks are added and removed. See fstab(5).
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>
proc            /proc           proc    defaults        0       0
# / was on /dev/sda6 during installation
UUID=4923760a-6e34-4678-b0ab-d06b151a2998 /               ext4    errors=remount-ro 0       1
# swap was on /dev/sda5 during installation
UUID=f0c3f9d6-39e2-4bf4-8d9c-03e28e2b8ad3 none            swap    sw              0       0
/dev/scd0       /media/cdrom0   udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0       0
/dev/fd0        /media/floppy0  auto    rw,user,noauto,exec,utf8 0       0
# none /proc/bus/usb usbfs devgid=126,devmode=664 0 0

=================== sda5: Location of files loaded by Grub: ===================
```

### Post by VMC on 2010-09-29
Yes, it appears the grub on the MBR is not right. Mine also points to a partition and not to a sector.

Its pointing somewhere inside sda7:
```
Grub 2 is installed in the MBR of /dev/sda and looks at sector 523349784
...
/dev/sda7 455,976,960 612,810,751 156,833,792 83 Linux
```

edit: It appears Maverick is on sda6, and Lucid is on sda7. Is that correct?

If so, you could try choot-ing to Maverick and install grub from that partition.
I install grub from various partitions all the time without errors. But it needs to be intact.

---

### Post by Quackers on 2010-09-29
Dougie, I don't know what to do about it (if it is a problem) but I notice 2 things with your boot script.
The first is that grub is looking at sector 523349784  for grub files, which is in sda2. No grub files are in sda2, they are in sda7.
Secondly can you navigate to /usr/sbin/grub-probe and confirm that grub-probe exists or not?
Either way I would have expected drs305's purge/re-install grub method should have worked. My grub was well messed up but that fixed it.......more than once.

---

### Post by VMC on 2010-09-29
> **Quackers said:**
> Dougie, I don't know what to do about it (if it is a problem) but I notice 2 things with your boot script.
The first is that grub is looking at sector 523349784  for grub files, which is in sda2. No grub files are in sda2, they are in sda7.
Secondly can you navigate to /usr/sbin/grub-probe and confirm that grub-probe exists or not?
Either way I would have expected drs305's purge/re-install grub method should have worked. My grub was well messed up but that fixed it.......more than once.

sda2 is the extended partition, and sector 523349784 falls inside logical partition sda7.

---

### Post by DougieFresh4U on 2010-09-29
> **Quackers said:**
> 
Secondly can you navigate to /usr/sbin/grub-probe and confirm that grub-probe exists or not?
Either way I would have expected drs305's purge/re-install grub method should have worked. My grub was well messed up but that fixed it.......more than once.
purge/re-install no luck

### Post by DougieFresh4U on 2010-09-30
Going to mark this 'Solved' although a solution to my issue was not found.
I went ahead and wipe the entire drive and first installed Win 7 and the latest Maverick daily and another Distro (triple boot) and what ever the problem with 'grub-pc' I had in my previous Maverick install seems to have not been an issue any longer (maybe new grub-pc is in latest daily)
I am happy to report that there were no problems with re-boot at any time during any of my latest installs
Thanks to all who participated in this thread and thanks to devs for working on this latest for Ubuntu

---
